channel.title: Browse items on Digital Derg: A Deep Map
channel.description: Recent content in Browse items on Digital Derg: A Deep Map

item.title: Derry and the Valley of the Foyle
Newtownstewart to Strabane on the Mourne
The Confluence of the Derg and Strule
"Salt fish, hides, cattle, and Irish hobby-horses"
"They conveyed us across to the rock one by one"
"This place I could not see, because I was unwilling to look into it"
"We passed over level ground through country pleasing enough to the eye"
"And out of there I crossed the sea"
"And there I embarked and crossed to Calais"
"I reached one of their towns called Tearmann"
"I reached the port of Dover where I saw Sir Gawain's head"
"Of all our Acquo Sanctificato, Lough Derg is the most celebrated"
"The purgatory is in this priory and there is a great deep lake"
"We arrived at Dublin where we embarked to cross to England"
"We returned by the road to king Ó Néill who received us very well and had great joy"
"And these two had orders to take me to the archbishop of Armagh"
"For nothing in the world would I abandon this journey"
"He rebuffed me very strongly and put great fear in me"
"He strongly advised and begged me to by no means enter the purgatory"
"I at once departed from him and went to the aforesaid town and from there sent to king Ó Néill"
"I had news that the king of England was in a great enclosed park"
"I would have to go through strange places inhabited by wild people"
"Saint Patrick had the reports set down in writing"
"The first prior of the aforesaid church"
"The prior advises them and if he sees that he cannot dissuade them from their intention"
"The whole island is surrounded by the waters of a large, very deep lake"
"As hard and wild as if they were beasts"
"Our Lord led him to a deserted place"
Laurent Vital hears of a pilgrimage to Donegal
Laurent Vital hears of a pilgrimage to Donegal
Laurent Vital hears of a pilgrimage to Donegal
Laurent Vital learns of Purgatory in Kinsale
Preface to Laurent Vital's visit to Kinsale in Ireland
Ramón de Perellós sets off for Saint Patrick's Purgatory
"Encompassed with high barren Mountains of difficult Ascent"
Geese and Swans on Lough Erne

Location: St. Brigid's Cross, Station Island

Location: Eagle's Rock Island, Lough Derg
Location: Derg Beg Island, Lough Derg
Location: Derg More Island, Lough Derg
A boat trip to Saints Island
Ease of travel to Lough Derg
Gazetteer description of the pilgrim crossing
Gazetteer description of view from lake shore
The afforestation of Lough Derg, and its geese
The quality of Lough Derg's road access
A disappointed description of Station Island
A traveller's guide to Lough Derg
Raymond de Perilleux and Lough Derg
The boatmen describes the "wine" of Purgatory
The Derg as it Flows to Castlederg
The History and Suppression of Lough Derg
The Pettigo to Lough Derg Road
The road from Pettigo to Donegal Town
The tradition of the Purgatorial Myth
The ugly terrain of Lough Derg
"A very strange Story hath been invented"
"Every Body knows how excessively the Irish are addicted to Pilgrimage"
"Lakes, Ponds, Wells, Trees, Stones, Crosses, Images, and Relicks"
"Such dismal and loansom Places are very apt to make frightful and melancholy Impessions upon the Minds for Weak and Ignorant"
"The Profit of a Ferry-Boat for wafting the Pilgrims over the Lake"
"When any Superstitious Place is defaced or demolished, they repair it"
A 54 page report lifting the suspension of John Goodwin
Letter, James Hamilton Jnr, Strabane, Co. Tyrone, to [Marquess of Abercorn, London]
Letter, James Hamilton Jnr, Strabane, Co. Tyrone, to Marquess of Abercorn, General Post Office, Dublin. (To be forwarded).
Letter, Robert Jamison, Baronscourt, to [Marquess of Abercorn, London].
Station Island, View from Ferry House
A Map of the Island of Purgatory
Lough Hill Bog Natural Heritage Area
"If there was no appearance of the pilgrim, he was given up for lost"
"St. Patrick most likely did visit the lake"
"The pilgrimage was suppressed and the cave destroyed"
"Rich in legendary, historic, and poetic association"
"There is no grandeur in the surrounding scenery"
"Two islands which have made it famous"
The Invasions of England and Ireland with al their civil ware since the conquest
Provincia Ultoniae. The Province of Ulster
The invasions of England and Ireland performed by John Speed, graven by Renold Elstrak
Nautical Chart Of Western Europe, Ca. 1400-1425
Map of the British Isles f. D1v
A Coloured Map of Great Britain and Ireland; Drawn on Vellum, in the period 1534-1546
Lough Golagh and Breesy Hill Special Area of Conservation
Tamur Bog Special Area of Conservation
Croaghonagh Bog Special Area of Conservation
Lough Eske and Ardnamona Wood Special Area of Conservation
Lough Nageage Special Area of Conservation
Termon McGrath Castle and Settlement Archaeological Survey
Inishgarve/Moylederg Island Crannog in Lough Eske Archaeological Survey
Castlederg to Lough Derg Pilgrim Path
Lines Written on St Patrick's Purgatory Lough Derg
Killeter Forest Bogs and Lakes Area of Special Scientific Interest (ASSI)
The source of the River Termon
Flora of the Lough Fad blanket bog to the south-west of Lough Derg
The conservation status of the Lough Fad blanket bog to the south-west of Lough Derg
Waterlife, flora and fauna of the Lough Fad blanket bog to the south-west of Lough Derg
Location and characteristics of the Lough Fad blanket bog to the south-west of Lough Derg
The presence of Arctic Char in the lake in the 1990s
Nesting sites of the Lesser Black-backed Gull and other birdlife on Inishgoosk
Seavog Mountain Pilgrim Road Archaeological Survey
"Towards the bottle-green, still / Shade of an oak"
"A rock, which, rising to the surface of the water, the breeze had exposed"
"He had swallowed up a very wicked man"
"I insisted that they should take me to an island"
"So ended my pilgrimage to Lough Derg"
"They told the marvellous incidents of the voyage to their listening friends"
"They would make a Jonas of me against my will"
"What we saw was the great serpent or fish"
"I at length reached the goal of my long-nourished desire"
"I gazed on the distant borders of the Lake"
"I had long experienced extreme anxiety to visit"
"Multitudes of the lower classes of the native Irish"
"So far shalt thou go, and no farther, and here shall thy proud waves be stayed"
"So long as the Church of Rome finds dupes"
"The lake itself changes its wonted blue into a dull brown colour"
"The pilgrims throng the shore opposite to the island"
"The unbroken stillness of this consecrated spot, was exchanged to bustle, noise, and jarring, of a countless multitude"
"A boy was standing like a ballet dancer poised on the rock"
"It was said stone dreams and animal sleeps and man is awake"
"The green islands that were his morning hope and his evening despair"
"The lake waves caught the concrete stilts of the Basilica"
"The little islands of Pan held in the crooked elbow of the lake"
"The middle of the island looked like the memory Of some village evicted by the Famine"
"The silver strands of the individual sometimes show"
"The turnips were a-sowing in the fields around Pettigo"
"Three boatloads of Dublin’s unemployed came in"
"We pray to ourself. The metal moon, unspent"
"For this is Lough Derg, St. Patrick’s Purgatory"
"Their hands push closed the doors that God holds open"
"They come to Lough Derg to fast and pray and beg"
"Women and men in bare feet turn again"
Bobby interrogates Jenny about her motivations
Jenny asks Bobby to go to Lough Derg
The impression of Lough Derg on Jenny and Bobby
Cardinal Michael Logue arrives at Station Island, 1913
Cardinal Michael Logue boards a boat at the Station Island ferry house, 1913
Pilgrims board their boats south-east of Station Island
View of Station Island from east shore
View of Station Island with boats and Friars' Island
A rower and Station Island with detail of rocks and water
Ferry house and view of Station Island
Penitential beds and view of lake
Pilgrims at the water station, north view
Pilgrims resting, and view of Sanctuary and shoreline
Rowing boats leaving the ferry house
The penitential beds with lake in background
View of Station Island with man in boat
A man in a boat with Station Island in the background
Photograph of a crowd at the Ferry House, Lough Derg, Co. Donegal
Photograph of Islets on Lough Derg south shore
Photograph of Lough Derg, Co. Donegal
Rowers in front of Friars' Island
Dutch Map of Ulster with Purgatory
"I do not think even Paddy could do much in protecting this lake"
"If I had my will, every otter-fisher should be solemnly ducked"
"In nine cases out of ten, the legend is simply an attempt made by an unlettered, but imaginative people, to account for natural appearances by supernatural agency"
"Shades of evening were adding solemnity to the landscape"
"This task was soon rendered less difficult by the rising of the moon"
"Though my letters are wild as the mountains in which they were written"
Fionn Mac Cumhaill and the worm of Lough Derg
The March of the Dead Maguires
The monster guards a crock of gold
"Eagles build in the islands of Lough Derg"
The brooks and rivulets of Templecarn
The extent of the Templecarn bogs
The mountains and hills of Templecarn
"A fantastic display of lightning which continued for nearly two hours"
"A mountain-locked lake that is just as secluded to-day as when Saint Patrick was attracted to its solitude"
"All around it is the glint and stir of water"
"Briars encompass it so thickly, and with such aggressiveness, one cannot approach within five or six feet of the mound"
"In going there they are answering the call for blood"
"In the end, all were obliged to return to the mainland and wait in the ferry house until the storm subsided"
"On prizing up a few loose stones, one exposes heaps of enormous bluebell bulbs, white and skull-like in shape"
"Recited in the open, while facing the airy spaciousness of mountain, sky and water"
"Rip Van Winkle whose experience is reversed"
"Shaking the very stars with their fusillades of Paters"
"The shaft on which this iron cross is set is precious, for it was salvaged from the lake"
"The stones become doubly slippery and the whole slope acquires a slithery and greasy surface"
"The very strangeness of the whole rite is like an old memory overlaid by time"
"There were boats at hand, and presence of mind, even among the onlookers, could have averted the tragedy"
"Unconcernedly the Easter sunshine poured down on that scene of desertion and decay"
"You will find instead only stumps, bumps, and conquering armies of weeds, cruel in their strength"
An omen of disaster prior to the 1795 accident
Burials on Friars' Island, in Templecarne, and further away
The journey of five women returning to Ireland in 1922
"A landscape that is usually a monotonous monochrome of either brown or grey tints"
"An ominous cloud appeared in the north"
"He always looked for the most inaccessible place in every district"
"No road, and not even a defined path, goes around the lake"
"One of the loneliest places on earth"
"Rolling, tawny bogland framed the blue water"
"Suddenly the far-famed waters of Lough Derg appear close at hand"
"The buildings give the impression of resting on the water"
"The Chair stands out sharply against the vandyke brown of hibernating heathers and the orange of wilted bracken"
"The Church never relinquished her hold upon the one grey rock"
"Those trackless hills enfold the lake as though to hide it."
"Tradition, when jealously guarded and carefully handed down is a reliable source of information"
Fair Lake, Lake of the Cave, Red Lake
The lake returns to the Diocese
The snake of Lough Patrick and Lough Peter
St Peter's Island and St Peter's Well
"One might well imagine it to be the golden portal to some bright realm"
"We left the Island with dry skies but still carrying our fast with us"
"We seemed to stand in a dim place where two worlds meet"
"There is a pool or lake saith he in the parts of Ulster that invironeth an Island"
Antonio Mannini is rowed to the island
Antonio Mannini prepares for his journey
How the demons came to Lough Derg
The miracle of the floating crosses
"Fainting heart and failing eye, Aching head and footstep weary"
"He who bathes, no Stygian flood need fear."
"That's the Red Lake unless I mistake"
"The fame of that place has been so scattered through European parts that it seems to go on wings"
Lough Derg in the Seventeenth Century
"Some dark spot in the midst of flowing silver"
"See ye not here this rock some power secureth"
"The fatal refuge of the frozen night"
"The water, the water of the seas and of lakes and of mist and rain, has all but made the Irish after its image"
"Without doubt to-day some pilgrim Roweth to this island shore."
A poetic description of the Dissolution of the Monasteries as a storm
A visit in the reign of Henry VIII
Fionn mac Cumhaill escapes the monster's belly
Saints Island is too close to shore
The acknowledgement of a rebaptised pilgrim
A description of Lough Derg and its topography
Interrogating the curse of St. Patrick
Knight Owen crosses to Saints Island
Otway muses on the metaphor of birds
St. Dabheoc's Seat and the old pilgrim road
The lough in the twelfth century
The northern shore of the lake
The origin of Lough Derg's name
"On the red tideless water is writ the destiny of Ireland"
A scriptural account of immersion in Lough Derg
A story of Ugolino's death on Station Island
An early description of the stations of Station Island
Another fictitious Lough Derg, Coronea, County Cavan
Fictitious Lough Derg in the Chapel of Monea, Diocese of Clogher
Full immersion at the water station
The business of ferrying pilgrims to Station Island
The journey from Pettigo to Lough Derg
The Rathnacross to Saints Island pilgrim road
Pilgrims being transported to Station Island on Lough Derg
